title: Watchdog warns banks of rising 'insider' threat
full_text:
The head of Australia's financial crimes watchdog has warned of a growing insider threat within the banking sector as it grapples with expanding mortgage fraud and gears up for a sweeping crackdown on money laundering across the accountancy, legal and real estate sectors.
AUSTRAC chief executive Brendan Thomas made his comments amid an ongoing collaboration between the watchdog and 10 of the nation's largest banks to determine how widespread mortgage fraud is across the sector and whether properties funded through the proceeds of crime should be seized.
The agency is also preparing, from July, to widen anti-money laundering investigations beyond the banking industry to cover a suite of new professional services to help tackle gangs linked to organised crime - in particular, the multibillion-dollar illicit tobacco industry.
"We have been speaking to banks for the best part of last year about insider threats, and it's something I know they're very focused on, and we've been encouraging them to focus on, not just in terms of mortgage fraud, but in terms of money laundering more generally," Thomas told The Australian Financial Review.
"We've seen a greater prevalence of insider threat in financial institutions, but the issue of mortgage fraud that we're looking at is the broader context of professional enablers and outside sources."
The Financial Review revealed earlier this year that Commonwealth Bank had found loan irregularities and potential fraud within its mortgage portfolio, identifying more than $1 billion in potential questionable activity.
Reviews conducted by other banks, which are focusing on issues such as AI-generated documents and the use of shell companies, have now uncovered about $3 billion in suspect mortgages across the sector.
Thomas declined to confirm the figure, as lenders within the Fintel Alliance - an AUSTRAC initiative to protect the financial sector from criminal exploitation - worked through "millions of points of data" to understand the scale of the problem. He said he hoped to "have some conclusions" in May.
But the financial crimes agency boss warned that while intelligence showed "a fair bit of illicit money being stored in Australian real estate", the bigger picture showed criminal gangs were taking an increasingly multifaceted approach to launder and hide the profits of serious transnational crimes such as tobacco and drugs smuggling.
A major legal reform coming into force on July 1 will, for the first time, expand anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing laws beyond the banking sector to professional services in the accounting and real estate world, as well as the legal profession and trust and company service providers.
AUSTRAC hopes to use the landmark shift as an opportunity to work more closely with so-called professional gatekeepers to disrupt profits fuelling crime by closing gaps that enable criminal networks to look legitimate - in particular those propping up a tobacco underworld estimated to be worth between $4.1 billion and $6.9 billion in illicit funds.
Accountants, lawyers and real estate agents were in a prime position to get early visibility of suspect activities, such as shell companies and false ownership facades, and report them, Thomas said.
Red flags included property purchases with no clear source of funds or multiple payees, or rapid or irregular mortgage pay-offs.
This was particularly relevant for the "significant volume of money" linked to the illegal tobacco trade, he said.
"We see a bunch of it coming into the Australian real estate market, but we also see a lot of it moving through corporate arrangements that try to obfuscate people's identity, so moving between different company structures," Thomas said.
Criminal networks used professional money laundering organisations to channel and hide their profits through multiple means.
"They're just constantly laundering through company structures, through real estate arrangements, through cash movements, through movements from cash into cryptocurrency, through bad trade arrangements," he said.
Thomas said the July reforms were less about catching bad actors already on the radar of law enforcement agencies and more focused on engaging with professional services to stop criminals at the front door.
summary: AUSTRAC warns of increasing insider threats in the banking sector amid rising mortgage fraud and upcoming anti-money laundering reforms targeting professional services.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: AUSTRAC is collaborating with major banks to assess the extent of mortgage fraud and insider threats.
A major legal reform will extend anti-money laundering laws to include accounting, legal, and real estate sectors from July 1.
Criminal networks are increasingly using professional services to launder money, particularly linked to the illicit tobacco trade.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights emerging risks and regulatory changes that may impact financial institutions and their operations.
